item.description: Germany's international development agency GIZ has tightened its control mechanisms in high-risk countries such as Syria and Ukraine following fraud cases in development projects in Yemen, the agency's chief spokesman Thorsten Schäfer-Gümbel told dpa on Wednesday. "There is a whole set of rules that have been changed and tightened.

item.title: Bolivia's government is 'stoking a civil war,' ex-president Evo Morales tells AFP
item.description: Former Bolivian president Evo Morales told AFP in an exclusive interview on Tuesday that the South American country's government is "stoking a civil war" with its "neoliberal politics." A: With all the neoliberal politics and the colonial state, they are stoking a civil war.

item.title: Argentina to return two rare maned wolves to the wild
item.description: By Lucila Sigal BUENOS AIRES, June 24 (Reuters) - Conservationists will return two maned wolves, rescued after the death of their mother, to the wild next month in Argentina, where the long-legged

item.title: Israeli attacks kill 2 in southern Lebanon despite ongoing Washington talks
item.description: Israeli defence minister says troops won’t withdraw from southern Lebanon as attacks slow but do not stop.

item.title: EU drafts support mission for Lebanese security forces
item.description: The European Union is planning a military and civilian support mission for Lebanon's armed forces and police aimed at helping the country extend state authority across its territory, according to a concept paper seen by dpa on Wednesday. The proposal, drafted by the EU's diplomatic service, envisages a mission lasting at least three years to improve the operational capabilities and readiness of Lebanon's security forces through advice, training and capacity-building.

item.title: Airstrike killed senior ISIS commander in Syria, CENTCOM says
item.description: A senior Islamic State leader was killed by an airstrike last week, Central Command announced on Wednesday.

item.title: Boy, 16, denies attempted murder at Norfolk school
item.description: The teenager appears in court after the stabbing of a 15-year-old girl in March.

item.title: Why Buckingham Palace Made the Rare Move of Canceling the Iconic Changing of the Guard This Week
item.description: "We take the well-being of our soldiers and military working horses very seriously," the Household Division said in a statement

item.title: US health deals cause trouble for Kenya govt
item.description: Washington's transactional approach to aid has sparked anger in Kenya, with pushback over deals for an Ebola quarantine centre and access to personal health data.But the idea of then building an Ebola facility just for US citizens on Kenyan territory sparked fear, as well as anger at the colonial undertones.

item.title: German body tightens checks on development projects after Yemen fraud
item.description: Germany's international development agency GIZ has tightened its control mechanisms in high-risk countries such as Syria and Ukraine following fraud cases in development projects in Yemen, the agency's chief spokesman Thorsten Schäfer-Gümbel told dpa on Wednesday. "There is a whole set of rules that have been changed and tightened.

item.title: Colombia’s leftwing candidate concedes election to Trump-endorsed millionaire
item.description: Leftist Iván Cepeda conceded to far-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella who won by razor-thin margin

item.title: An Auditor Filmed Inside a Council Building, Got Confronted by Police, and Refused to Back Down — X Is Split on Who Was Right
item.description: The officer asked to see his footage. He declined unless threatened with arrest.

item.title: Military deployed in Sri Lanka to fight surging dengue outbreak
item.description: The Sri Lankan military has been deployed to help fight a spiralling dengue outbreak, after more than 1,000 people were infected in a single day.

item.title: Berlin memorial commemorates Jehovah's Witnesses persecuted by Nazis
item.description: A memorial to commemorate Jehovah's Witnesses persecuted by Germany's Nazi regime was inaugurated in a Berlin park on Wednesday, 81 years after the end of World War II. “This memorial is dedicated to the people who suffered bitter injustice and yet time and again demonstrated humanity,” said Julia Klöckner, president of the Bundestag, during the unveiling ceremony in the Tiergarten park.

item.title: Suspected Russian-linked gas-supply sabotage prompts raids in Germany
item.description: German federal prosecutors are investigating a Russian national on suspicion of attempting to sabotage Germany's gas supply following the start of Russia's war against Ukraine, with a former subsidiary of state-owned Russian energy group Gazprom at the centre of the case. On Wednesday, officers searched premises belonging to the suspect and a second person, who is not a suspect, in Berlin, Germany's supreme prosecuting authority said on Wednesday.

item.title: First ships pass through Strait of Hormuz under UN evacuation scheme
item.description: The first ships have passed through the Strait of Hormuz under a UN-backed operation to evacuate thousands of seafarers and restore maritime traffic after weeks of disruption, the International Maritime Organization (IMO) said on Wednesday. The UN shipping agency announced plans on Tuesday to evacuate 11,000 seafarers from the region.

item.title: Italian police divers find huge cannabis stash in sea cave off celebrity holiday island
item.description: Police divers have discovered more than half a million dollars worth of hashish hidden in a cave on the picturesque Italian island of Ponza.

item.title: Body Found After Cruise Ship Crew Member Went Overboard Off the Coast of Scotland
item.description: The 'Hebridean Princess' crew member fell overboard near the Scottish town of Oban on June 16, the cruise line reports

item.title: British Airways pilot who raped girl, 12, jailed
item.description: Kwame Yeboah from Reading travelled to meet the victim in Wirral after they first messaged on Instagram.

item.title: Most SUV Buyers Unmoved by Safety Risks to Cyclists and Pedestrians, New Study Finds
item.description: A new study suggests most SUV buyers are not swayed by warnings that larger vehicles pose greater risks to cyclists and pedestrians. Researchers say awareness campaigns alone may not change behavior and tougher measures could be needed.

item.title: Berlin unveils monument to Jehovah's Witnesses murdered by Nazis
item.description: Germany on Wednesday unveiled a monument to Jehovah's Witnesses persecuted and murdered by the Nazis -- a group that experts say has been historically overlooked in commemorations of the Holocaust.Around 14,000 Jehovah's Witnesses –- at least 10,700 Germans and 2,700 from European countries occupied by Germany -- were persecuted by the Nazis, mostly in the form of imprisonment.

item.title: France sees first Ebola case detected in Europe during Congo outbreak
item.description: France has confirmed the first Ebola case detected in Europe since the start of the outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, health authorities said on Wednesday. A humanitarian aid doctor who returned from the Central African country tested positive for the virus, the French Health Ministry said.

item.title: Israeli attacks on Gaza and occupied West Bank kill two, including child
item.description: Health officials at Nasser Medical Complex tell Al Jazeera a 12-year-old child was killed in al-Mawasi, Gaza.

item.title: 'Inhumane': Gaza flotilla activists recount Israeli detention ordeal
item.description: Cracked bones, humiliation, sexual assault: Pro-Palestinian activists recounted the abuse they say they suffered from Israeli authorities for taking part in a Gaza-bound aid flotilla last month, which has sparked multiple investigations and international outcry. France, Italy and Australia have launched probes into the allegations of abuse -- which Israeli authorities deny -- after more than 430 activists from around the world were detained during the latest attempt by an aid flotilla to break t

item.title: Volunteer who helped clean up severely polluted creek now faces criminal investigation
item.description: The central question seems to be whether the cleanup involved enough dredging.

item.title: Moscow missile strikes ‘threaten Putin’s grip on power’
item.description: Ukraine’s strikes on Moscow are threatening Vladimir Putin’s grip on power, Nato officials believe.

item.title: Funds raised to return man's body after crash
item.description: Joseph O'Sullivan, from Stoke Heath, died in a road crash while on holiday in Bali.

item.title: Switzerland starts talks to acquire non-US air defence system
item.description: ZURICH, June 24 (Reuters) - Switzerland has started contract negotiations with manufacturers from France, Israel and South Korea for a ‌second air defence system following delays to its order of U. ‌Patriot missile systems, the government said on Wednesday.

item.title: Microsoft, Germany's BKA and Europol take down 200 cybercrime servers
item.description: Investigators from Europol, Germany's Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA) and US tech giant Microsoft have dealt a major blow to organized cybercrime, taking down more than 200 command-and-control servers used by criminal hackers to remotely control infected devices. At the same time, criminal control over more than 18,000 identified victim computers worldwide was severed, they said on Wednesday.

item.title: Mom Attacked by Shark Is Brought Out of Medically Induced Coma for the First Time and Tells Family, ‘I Love You’
item.description: "This is such a positive first step and gives us hope for Leah’s long term recovery," said Leah Stewart's brother

item.title: Why Accra slavery reparatory justice meeting matters
item.description: From apology to action: the renewed push for slavery reparations amid questions over delivery.

item.title: Berlin memorial commemorates Jehovah's Witnesses persecuted by Nazis
item.description: A memorial to commemorate Jehovah's Witnesses persecuted by Germany's Nazi regime was inaugurated in a Berlin park on Wednesday, 81 years after the end of World War II. “This memorial is dedicated to the people who suffered bitter injustice and yet time and again demonstrated humanity,” said Julia Klöckner, president of the Bundestag, during the unveiling ceremony in the Tiergarten park.

item.title: Paris's Cirque d'Hiver begins renovation to restore its original splendour
item.description: Twenty ornamental frescoes from the Second Empire are to be restored over twelve to eighteen months. The seating, the polychrome ceiling canvas and the stained-glass windows will also be refurbished.View on euronews

item.title: Image of Netanyahu supporting Argentina at World Cup is AI-generated
item.description: An image spreading on social media purports to show Benjamin Netanyahu supporting the Argentinian men's national football team during the side's 2026 FIFA World Cup match against Austria. But the visual is fake, with OpenAI's image verification tool assessing it was generated with the company's artificial intelligence programs. The Israeli prime minister was hosting meetings in Jerusalem the same day the June 22 game took place in Dallas, Texas.

item.title: First whales killed as Iceland restarts commercial hunt
item.description: Iceland resumes commercial whaling after a two-year pause, with two fin whales processed at the Hvalur facility, reigniting controversy and protests.

item.title: France reports Ebola case in doctor returning from Congo
item.description: PARIS, June 24 () - A doctor who recently returned to France from a humanitarian mission in the Democratic Republic of Congo ‌has tested positive for Ebola, marking the country's first confirmed ‌case linked to the current outbreak, the health ministry said on Wednesday. The patient has been ​placed in isolation and health authorities are tracing contacts, the ministry said in a statement, adding that the risk to the wider European population was low.

item.title: Son of Norway's crown princess appeals convictions in rape trial
item.description: Marius Borg Høiby, the 29-year-old eldest son of Norwegian Crown Princess Mette-Marit, has formally appealed the court verdict handed down against him last week, his lawyer told Norwegian news agency NTB on Wednesday. An Oslo court sentenced Høiby to four years in prison for 34 offences.

item.title: Woman, 42, Says She Was Tied Up and Doused with Gasoline Before Catching Fire in Escape Room Challenge: Prosecutors
item.description: Sources said the woman, who suffered burns to 40% of her body, has been in a "living hell" since the alleged incident

item.title: Progressive candidate concedes Colombian presidential election to Trump-endorsed outsider
item.description: Progressive candidate Iván Cepeda on Wednesday conceded Colombia’s presidential election to Abelardo de la Espriella, a conservative outsider who was endorsed by U.S. President Donald Trump. Election results showed de la Espriella, a businessman and lawyer who had never run for office, defeated Cepeda, a lawmaker, by 1 percentage point, or nearly 251,000 votes.

item.title: Why immigrants are leaving South Africa and the country is on edge ahead of a June 30 'deadline'
item.description: Thousands of African immigrants are leaving South Africa because of a rise in anti-migration anger that sparked attacks against foreigners. The attacks have coincided with a series of protests and marches in recent months by anti-immigration groups, who have set what they are calling a June 30 deadline for people in the country illegally to leave and the government to take action against what they say is a growing problem of illegal immigration in Africa's biggest economy. Meanwhile, thousands of immigrants have gathered in temporary shelters, near embassies and elsewhere citing fear of attacks.

item.title: Businessmen poisoned by Romanian woman posing as sex worker
item.description: A Romanian woman who posed as a sex worker has admitted killing two wealthy businessmen by spiking their drinks to steal from them when they invited her into their homes.

item.title: Uyghur Forced Labor Risks Are Shifting to Australia and Japan, Report Says
item.description: Countries without forcedlabor import prohibitions not only remain exposed to forced labor-tainted goods, the Uyghur Human Rights Project warned, but they may also attract them.

item.title: Ordinary Russians can’t escape the blame for Putin’s wars of aggression
item.description: More than four years into Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, the West is still arguing with a Russia it would prefer to exist. Faced with overwhelming evidence of Russian aggression, many politicians, academics and journalists remain caught between two powerful impulses: fear and denial.

item.title: Ebola outbreak is still outpacing response, WHO's Tedros says
item.description: DAKAR, June 24 () - The Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo ‌and Uganda is continuing to outpace response ‌efforts, World Health Organization chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said ​on Wednesday. "Despite the good progress we have made, we still face major challenges, and the outbreak is continuing to outpace the response," ‌Tedros told a ⁠press conference.

item.title: UNICEF, Gavi launch call to speed Bundibugyo Ebola vaccine access
item.description: June 24 () - UNICEF and Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance, said on Wednesday they are seeking information from vaccine ‌developers and manufacturers on plans to develop a vaccine against ‌the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola. The move follows Gavi's commitment of up to $40 million ​to support faster vaccine access, including funding to help scale up manufacturing and ensure doses are available if candidates prove effective.

item.title: Complex crypto networks are used to facilitate financial flow, funding Houthis' war
item.description: Analysts and local sources describe a system built on Tether, temporary wallets, weakly regulated exchanges, and cash-out networks on the ground. In the basement of a dilapidated building in Yemen’s capital, Sanaa, Nasser Sharhan, 46, sits under the dim light of a candle.

item.title: Russia is still inching forward in eastern Ukraine, but experts say claims of progress are exaggerated
item.description: Russia is still inching forward in eastern Ukraine’s Donetsk region, but outside experts and Ukrainian soldiers say Russia’s claims of advancement are exaggerated.

item.title: France reports first Ebola case in patient who returned from humanitarian visit to DRC
item.description: A patient in France has tested positive for Ebola after returning from a humanitarian mission in an area where the virus is circulating in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

item.title: 'You have to work': Riders brave Rome heat for survival
item.description: Delivery riders dashed around Rome in the hottest hours of the day on Wednesday as activists with a thermal imaging camera detected blistering temperatures in the streets.The union is also trying to ensure that the riders, many of whom are self-employed, are given contracts that mean they could be paid even if they do not work in the hottest hours.

item.title: How the world views Keir Starmer’s resignation
item.description: With the prospect of seven prime ministers in the last ten years, some see Downing Street as a revolving door, and Britain as ‘ungovernable’

item.title: Experts: AI turbocharging cybercrime, Germany a hotspot for ransomware
item.description: Germany faces turbulent times ahead due to the growing threat of AI-driven cyberattacks, the head of the country's cybersecurity authority warned at a conference in Potsdam outside Berlin on Wednesday. Claudia Plattner, president of the Federal Office for Information Security (BSI), said a "new era of cybersecurity" had begun to some extent.

item.title: Counselling centres report surge in anti-Muslim hatred across Germany
item.description: Staff at counselling centres for Muslims affected by hate crimes are reporting a growing loss of inhibition and increasing brutality in anti-Muslim incidents, according to a new situation report by the Claim network released on Wednesday. Of the 4,096 anti-Muslim incidents documented by the Claim network last year, 214 involved bodily harm.

item.title: The homilies and addresses of Pope Leo XIV are now coming to English readers
item.description: A collection of early public writings by the future Pope Leo XIV will be published this fall for the first time in English. “Freedom Under Grace: Reflections on the Spiritual Tradition That Formed Me” is scheduled for Sept. 15, according to Image Books, an imprint of the Penguin Random House Christian Publishing Group. Released in Italian by the Vatican Publishing House earlier this year, “Freedom from Grace” features homilies, addresses and other works by Robert Prevost, when he was prior general of the Order of Saint Augustine from 2001 to 2013.

item.title: Vatican begins 5-year restoration of Raphael Loggia, used by popes and presidents
item.description: One of the most intricately decorated parts of the Vatican’s Apostolic Palace, a passageway walked by popes and presidents and attributed to Renaissance master Raphael, is getting its first major face-lift in over 500 years. The Vatican Museums on Wednesday announced the start of a five-year, $5.5 million project to clean and restore the Raphael Loggia, a 65-meter (yard) long, 4-meter (yard) wide corridor that is considered one of the highest expressions of Renaissance figurative art.

item.title: UN commission accuses Israel of deliberately shooting children in Gaza, repeats genocide claim
item.description: A team of independent experts commissioned by the United Nations accused Israel of deliberately shooting children in Gaza and repeated its accusation that Israel has committed genocide in the territory. Israel vociferously denies claims that it committed genocide during its 2 1/2 year war in Gaza. The Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory, an investigative body operating under the U.N. Human Rights Council, said in its report Tuesday that roughly 30% of the Palestinians killed from October 2023 to October 2025 — more than 20,000 in total — were children.

item.title: American boy attacked by shark in Bahamas while swimming with brother
item.description: The boy was on a tour of the Bahamas' Exuma Cays with his family when the attack occurred, the Royal Bahamas Police Force said.

item.title: Older people: Redefining humanity
item.description: The world is aging at an unprecedented rate. The number of people aged 60 and older is expected to reach 1.4 billion by 2030.

item.title: What U.S. Special Operations Veterans Are Learning From Ukraine's War
item.description: Ukraine built more than drones. It created a wartime innovation ecosystem that U.S. military veterans say America should study.

item.title: A Con Artist, a Crooked Reporter, and the Truth Behind 60 of Hitler's Personal Diaries
item.description: The forger confused his gothic letters, used post-war glue, and aged the covers with tea. The real mystery is why anyone believed him at all.

item.title: What does Pakistan stand to gain from helping broker the US-Iran deal?
item.description: Pakistan sees strategic opportunity in the US-Iran thaw, including trade, energy access and renewed regional relevance.

item.title: First Assad family member, Bashar's cousin, stands trial in Syria
item.description: The trial of Wassim al-Assad, a cousin of former Syrian president Bashar al-Assad, began on Wednesday at the Palace of Justice in Damascus, marking the first court case involving a member of the al-Assad family since the fall of the former regime. He faces charges of forming armed groups, drug trafficking, kidnapping, and extortion, according to the court.

item.title: Train driver killed in UK crash passed red signal, investigators say
item.description: A train driver killed in a crash in south-eastern England passed a red signal moments before the collision, investigators said on Wednesday. Shaun Burton, 60, died when the train he was driving hit the back of a stationary train run by the same operator.

item.title: Qatar expects LNG output to return to normal within weeks, PM says
item.description: Qatar expects to return to normal liquefied natural gas (LNG) production "within a few weeks," Prime Minister Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani told the Financial Times in an interview published on Wednesday. He said an exception was the Ras Laffan gas facility, which was damaged by Iranian attacks during the war and most recently by an explosion.

item.title: Kenya signs deal with Chinese firm for airport upgrade
item.description: The agreement comes as Nairobi faces increasing competition from Ethiopia and Rwanda, which are jostling for power as East Africa’s main travel hub.

item.title: IDF surrounds Hezbollah fighters in Tebnit as fears rise of possible kidnapping attempt
item.description: Israeli forces have sealed tunnel entrances and instructed troops to operate in small units, amid fears that underground infrastructure could be used for ambush or abduction attempts. Amid growing concerns over a potential kidnapping attempt in southern Lebanon, Israeli security officials said Tuesday that senior officials reviewed the situation involving dozens of Hezbollah terrorists trapped in the village of Tebnit.

item.title: The New Energy War: Why The AI Grid Is The New Battleground
item.description: Russia can't bomb the West, so it's hacking it. How AI data centers and Texas's ERCOT grid became the next cyberwarfare battleground.

item.title: 15 People Report ‘Violently Throwing Up’ and Other Norovirus Symptoms After Dining at Buffet Restaurant
item.description: One customer said they had the "worst food poisoning symptoms" they had "ever experienced"

item.title: IDF strikes four new Hamas rocket-firing positions built in Gaza post-ceasefire
item.description: Hamas is now working harder than before to redevelop rocket firing capabilities against Israel to intimidate the Jewish state into heeding its demands. The IDF on Wednesday struck four rocket-firing positions that Hamas had constructed in Gaza after the October 2025 ceasefire.

item.title: One in five people in Germany lives alone, figures show
item.description: Some 17. 3 million people in Germany lived alone last year, equivalent to roughly one in five of the population (20.

item.title: Counselling centres report surge in anti-Muslim hatred across Germany
item.description: Staff at counselling centres for Muslims affected by hate crimes are reporting a growing loss of inhibition and increasing brutality in anti-Muslim incidents, according to a new situation report by the Claim network released on Wednesday. Of the 4,096 anti-Muslim incidents documented by the Claim network last year, 214 involved bodily harm.

item.title: Report: Suspected terrorist tunnel found near East Jerusalem crossing
item.description: Israeli police have discovered a tunnel about 25 metres below ground near a checkpoint in East Jerusalem that was presumably intended for terrorist purposes, the Haaretz newspaper reported on Wednesday, citing security authorities. It said the tunnel had been dug from the West Bank towards Israeli territory.

item.title: Keiko Fujimori's expected victory returns divisive dynasty to Peru
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: China’s 360 says it has developed tools to match Anthropic’s Mythos
item.description: BEIJING, June 24 (Reuters) - Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 Security Technology has developed what it calls a domestic answer to Anthropic's Mythos, it said on Wednesday, casting the U. model as a strategic cyber capability that China could not afford to lack.

item.title: One in five people in Germany lives alone, figures show
item.description: Some 17. 3 million people in Germany lived alone last year, equivalent to roughly one in five of the population (20.

item.title: Man who could not swim drowns in Danube after disappearing under water
item.description: A 26-year-old man who could not swim has drowned while bathing in the Danube near Regensburg, police said on Wednesday. The man disappeared beneath the surface within seconds on Tuesday in the area of the Nibelungen Bridge, police said.

item.title: Germany, France, UK express concern over China's activities off Taiwan
item.description: Germany, France and the United Kingdom on Wednesday jointly expressed concern over recent Chinese activities off Taiwan's east coast. "These actions threaten regional stability and the freedom of navigation and safety of international shipping," the three countries’ representative offices in Taiwan said in a joint statement.

item.title: France detects first Ebola case outside Africa in current outbreak
item.description: France on Wednesday announced its first confirmed case of Ebola identified on its territory, a doctor who had flown back from the Democratic Republic of Congo, which is fighting a major outbreak.The health ministry said it had identified "a first positive case of Ebola virus disease on national territory".

item.title: ‘My Office Is 35 Degrees’: Bus Driver’s Heatwave Video Goes Viral as Debate Over Working Conditions Heats Up
item.description: A video of a bus driver struggling through extreme heat inside his vehicle has gone viral on social media. The video prompted discussion online about working conditions for public transport employees during the United Kingdom’s ongoing heatwave. The footage was shared on X by @benonwine. In the video, the driver appears to be speaking to…

item.title: UK swelters in another record-breaking heatwave
item.description: A man cycles past the Bank of England as Britain experiences record temperaturesTemperatures are expected to reach 39°C (102°F) this week.

item.title: Israel deliberately targeting children in ongoing genocide against Palestinians, UN commission finds
item.description: Israeli forces continue to commit genocide against Palestinians by deliberately targeting children in the Gaza Strip, an independent United Nations Commission of Inquiry has found.

item.title: Expert panel calls for mobile phone ban in German schools up to age 13
item.description: An expert commission has recommended banning the private use of mobile phones in German schools nationwide during lessons and breaks for all pupils up to seventh grade, or age 12-13, according to the panel's published recommendations. The recommendation comes from the expert commission on "Child and Youth Protection in the Digital World," which said the ban should be enshrined uniformly in all school laws across the country.

item.title: Turkey working on legislation to speed up militant PKK's disbandment, Erdogan says
item.description: ANKARA, June 24 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan said on Wednesday that work was underway on a legal framework that would speed up the disbandment of the militant Kurdistan Workers Party (

item.title: Germany carries out raids in sabotage investigation related to former Gazprom unit
item.description: German prosecutors said they carried out raids Wednesday in an investigation of a suspected attempt to disrupt the country's gas supply, connected to an opaque maneuver in 2022 involving what was Russian energy giant Gazprom's German unit. Federal prosecutors said police searched the premises in Berlin of a suspect and another person who isn't under investigation, along with those of an unidentified company in Frankfurt. The suspect, a Russian citizen whose name wasn't released, is being investigated on suspicion of being an accessory to violating investment rules in Germany's foreign trade law and an accessory to attempted anticonstitutional sabotage, prosecutors said in a statement.

item.title: Iranian agents lived in Australia before directing attacks on Sydney and Melbourne, spy chief says
item.description: Iran’s Revolutionary Guard used two agents who once lived in Australia to direct arson attacks against Jewish targets in Sydney and Melbourne, the head of Australia's domestic spy agency said on Wednesday. “Iran continues to view Australia as a legitimate target for covertly directed acts of violence,” Mike Burgess, director-general of the Australian Security Intelligence Organization (ASIO), said.

item.title: Ukraine wants naval drones that can detain ships in the Black Sea rather than sink them, commander says
item.description: Policing missions would expand the role of Ukrainian naval drones, which have historically been used to strike Russian ships.

item.title: 'Blind rage': Man on trial over fatal attack on German train conductor
item.description: A man accused of fatally assaulting a train conductor during a ticket inspection in western Germany went on trial on Wednesday nearly five months after the attack. The 26-year-old Greek national is alleged to have punched the conductor in the head during a dispute aboard a regional train near the town of Landstuhl on February 2.

item.title: Germany is Europe's top ransomware target, expert says
item.description: Germany is the European country most affected by ransomware attacks, a German cybersecurity expert has said, adding that the incidents often trace back to Russia. Christian Dörr, a cybersecurity expert at the Hasso Plattner Institute (HPI) in Potsdam, made the remarks ahead of a national cybersecurity conference in the city on Wednesday.

item.title: Premises linked to former Gazprom subsidiary searched in Germany
item.description: German officers are raiding premises linked to a former subsidiary of Russian state-owned energy giant Gazprom on suspicion of attempted sabotage and complicity in violations of the Foreign Trade Act, prosecutors said on Wednesday. Federal prosecutors are searching premises in Berlin and at a company in Frankfurt in connection with the former energy group Gazprom Germania, the Federal Public Prosecutor's Office in Karlsruhe said.

item.title: France confirms first Ebola case linked to Congo outbreak
item.description: France has confirmed its first Ebola case linked to the disease's outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the French Health Ministry said on Wednesday. A humanitarian aid doctor who returned from the central African country tested positive, it said.

item.title: Russian-occupied Donetsk reports 3 deaths following Ukrainian attack
item.description: At least three people have been killed by Ukrainian drone attacks in the Russian-occupied part of eastern Ukraine, the occupation authorities reported on Wednesday. Ivan Prikhodko, the Moscow-installed mayor of Horlivka, in the Donetsk region, said on Telegram that a petrol station was damaged and at least one car was destroyed in the attacks involving remote-controlled drones.

item.title: German naval ambitions suffer setback as warship order axed
item.description: Germany on Wednesday pulled the plug on its largest naval order in decades after the anti-submarine warship project suffered delays, a setback for the country's ambitions to overhaul its military.Germany ordered four F126 anti-submarine frigates in 2020 from Dutch group Damen Navel, with the price set at 10 billion euros (then $11.2 billion), with the deal later expanded to include two more vessels.

item.title: London police to extend use of live facial recognition, drones
item.description: London's Metropolitan Police force said Wednesday it will expand the use of drones, artificial intelligence and live facial recognition technology, which has been criticised by rights campaigners.Civil liberties campaigning group Big Brother Watch slammed the live facial recognition expansion as an "alarming escalation of an intrusive technology". aks/jkb/phz

item.title: Meet the Man Facing Investigation for Removing 200 Bags of Trash From Polluted River
item.description: Cleaning up a polluted river sounds like the kind of thing officials would thank people for. But a UK barrister and environmental campaigner is now under investigation for helping to organize a volunteer cleanup that removed about 200 bags of trash, branches, weeds, and silt from a waterway. Paul Powlesland, founder of the River Roding …

item.title: French health ministry confirms Ebola virus in patient who worked in Congo
item.description: A positive case of Ebola virus has been identified in France in a patient traveling back from Congo, the French Ministry of Health said Wednesday. The individual, who has not been identified, returned from a humanitarian mission in one of the virus transmission zones in Congo and was taken into care at a specialized facility in France. The person is in stable condition, the ministry said.

item.title: Australia spy chief warns of Iran terror threat
item.description: An Australian citizen living in Iran who was a senior member of its Revolutionary Guards orchestrated a major antisemitic firebomb attack in Sydney, Australia's spy chief said Wednesday.An Iran-based Australian citizen orchestrated the 2024 firebombing of a Bondi restaurant, Lewis' Continental Kitchen, in the first major antisemitic attack in Australia, he said.

item.title: Premises linked to former Gazprom subsidiary searched in Germany
item.description: German officers are raiding premises linked to a former subsidiary of Russian state-owned energy giant Gazprom on suspicion of attempted sabotage and complicity in violations of the Foreign Trade Act, prosecutors said on Wednesday. Federal prosecutors are searching premises in Berlin and at a company in Frankfurt in connection with the former energy group Gazprom Germania, the authority said.

item.title: Adidas, Uniqlo, Calvin Klein adverts banned over ‘unclear’ recycled claims
item.description: The UK’s Advertising Standards Agency (ASA) has said Google adverts promoting recycled shoes and clothing must not appear again after challenging Adidas, Uniqlo and Calvin Klein about claims made.

item.title: Moscow Oil Refinery Hit By Drone Attacks Is Unlikely To Resume Production This Year: Reuters
item.description: Ukrainian strikes have knocked out a significant part of Moscow's oil refining capacity, triggering oil product shortages and fuel price increases.

item.title: EU moves to strengthen Europol against rising digital, cross-border crime
item.description: The European Commission on Wednesday unveiled measures to enable Europol, the ‌EU's law enforcement agency, to respond ‌more quickly and effectively to cross-border and increasingly digital ​crime. • Europol warned last year that organised crime gangs were turning to AI-powered scams to target victims. • The Commission said Europol will build ⁠a sovereign cloud infrastructure and create a shared data space to allow investigators to collaborate ​more easily ​on joint cases.

item.title: Germany is Europe's top ransomware target, expert says
item.description: Germany is the European country most affected by ransomware attacks, a German cybersecurity expert has said, adding that the incidents often trace back to Russia. Christian Dörr, a cybersecurity expert at the Hasso Plattner Institute (HPI) in Potsdam, made the remarks ahead of a national cybersecurity conference in the city on Wednesday.

item.title: Ukraine’s latest long-range strikes on Russia hit a major natural gas plant and satellite centers
item.description: Ukrainian forces struck a major natural gas processing plant and two key satellite communications centers in their latest nighttime attack on Russia, Ukraine’s General Staff said Wednesday. The operation was part of Ukraine’s aerial campaign targeting energy facilities and military industries that has intensified as Kyiv builds bigger and better long-range weapons to defeat Russia’s all-out invasion, now in its fifth year. The overnight attack hit the Orenburg Gas Processing Plant, which is part of a complex that also houses the only helium plant in Russia, the General Staff said in a statement on the Telegram messaging app.

item.title: Germany scraps F126 frigate program, pivots to MEKO warships amid cost and contractor chaos
item.description: Germany Navy leaders have already blessed the fallback ships as capable of meeting NATO capability requirements, but an alliance deadline looms.

item.title: France confirms 1st Ebola case linked to outbreak in the DRC
item.description: Officials say it was detected quickly and there's no indication of local spread.

item.title: France confirms first Ebola case in doctor returning from DR Congo
item.description: The patient is in isolation, following strict biosafety protocols to prevent risk of spread in France.

item.title: EU Commission aims to better fight cross-border organized crime
item.description: The European Commission aims to better fight cross-border organized crime and terrorism by improving the cooperation between national authorities and the European Union's law enforcement and judicial agencies. "Criminals are highly adept at exploiting the opportunities of the digital realm, operating effectively across borders without limitations," EU Commission Vice President Henna Virkkunen said on Wednesday.

item.title: Germany scraps F126 frigate project over cost concerns, delays
item.description: Germany has cancelled a multibillion-euro order for six new F126 frigates from a Dutch shipmaker, the Defence Ministry said on Wednesday, citing delays and rising costs. Main contractor Damen Schelde Naval Shipbuilding (DSNS) was due to deliver the first frigate in mid-2028, with the rest to follow by 2033.

item.title: North Korean soldier in custody after crossing DMZ into South Korea
item.description: A North Korean soldier was taken into custody after crossing the heavily fortified Military Demarcation Line separating the two Koreas, Seoul's military said Wednesday.

item.title: North Korean soldier crosses border in suspected defection, report says
item.description: A North Korean soldier has been taken into custody after crossing the Demilitarized Zone into South Korea in a suspected defection, the Yonhap news agency says.

item.title: Pakistani rights activist Mahrang Baloch sentenced to life in prison
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: Ukraine strikes knock out power in key city in Russian-occupied Crimea
item.description: Sevastopol's Moscow-installed governor warns there will be no electricity in some areas until the evening.

item.title: France confirms first Ebola case
item.description: More than 260 people in the Democratic Republic of Congo are known to have died during the current outbreak.

item.title: Japan says that China has detained 2 of its citizens suspected of smuggling banned items
item.description: China has detained two Japanese citizens suspected of smuggling items prohibited from import or export, a Japanese official said Wednesday, in a case reportedly linked to rare earths, critical materials that are largely controlled by Beijing. Japans's Chief Cabinet Secretary Minoru Kihara told reporters in Tokyo that Japanese consular offices in Shenyang and Dalian were notified by the Chinese customs authorities that the two Japanese nationals were detained, one on May 18 and another one a week later, “in the same alleged case.” Kihara said the two were in good health, but refused to give further details about them or the case, on grounds of their privacy and the ongoing investigation.

item.title: The Islamic legacy built into Gaudí’s most famous designs
item.description: The Barcelona architect was a devout Catholic, but his work was heavily influenced by Moorish techniques.

item.title: Sources: Opening of Stuttgart station delayed to 2031 in new setback
item.description: Stuttgart's new train station is not due to open before the end of 2031, sources told dpa on Wednesday, saying the railway operator has pushed back the launch of the controversial underground project by another five years and for the fifth time since construction began in 2010. The new main station in the south-western German city, which is home to major firms including Mercedes and Bosch, has become the object of ridicule among Germans and is considered emblematic of the country's struggle to u

item.title: Four Gaza aid flotilla activists released from Libya detention
item.description: Global Sumud Flotilla group says six others remain in detention and are expected to be released within 24 hours.

item.title: Image of ex-Thai PM Thaksin with sister in Dubai is AI-generated
item.description: Weeks after former Thai prime minister Thaksin Shinawatra was officially freed by a royal pardon in June 2026, social media users in Thailand shared an AI-generated image that purports to show him visiting his exiled sister Yingluck Shinawatra in Dubai. A close analysis found clues the image was inauthentic, while detection tools indicated it was created using Google's AI models.

item.title: Sources: Opening of Stuttgart station delayed to 2031 in new setback
item.description: Stuttgart's new train station is not due to open before the end of 2031, sources told dpa on Wednesday, saying the railway operator has pushed back the launch of the controversial underground project by another five years and for the fifth time since construction began in 2010. The new main station in the south-western German city, which is home to major firms including Mercedes and Bosch, has become the object of ridicule among Germans and is considered emblematic of the country's struggle to u

item.title: Premier says China’s tech advancements an 'opportunity' for the world, not a threat
item.description: China’s Premier Li Qiang on Wednesday defended the country’s technological advancements as an opportunity for the world rather than a threat. Li also said the country’s heavy state subsidies were not the main reason for the rapid rise of its high-tech industries, at a time when Western officials have complained that China’s state support for industries from artificial intelligence to electric vehicles has provided an unfair competitive edge. China’s No. 2 leader made the remarks in his speech at the opening plenary of the World Economic Forum’s Annual Meeting of the New Champions, known as the “Summer Davos,” held this week in the northeastern Chinese coastal city of Dalian.

item.title: Pakistani activists given life in prison on riot incitement charges
item.description: Two human rights activists in Pakistan were sentenced to life in prison for allegedly inciting a mob that led to the death of a paramilitary soldier.

item.title: U.N. agency to evacuate 11,000 sailors stranded by Hormuz crisis
item.description: The United Nations' maritime organization said it will begin evacuating thousands of sailors stranded for months in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: UK, France, Germany raise alarm about Chinese activities off eastern Taiwan
item.description: TAIPEI, June 24 (Reuters) - Britain, France and Germany raised the alarm on Wednesday over recent Chinese activities off the east coast of Taiwan, where ‌China has mounted coast guard patrols, saying they threaten regional stability and freedom of navigation. China, ‌which views democratically governed Taiwan as its own territory, earlier in June sent coast guard ships into the waters off the island's ​east coast for what it called a "special maritime traffic law-enforcement operation", angerin

item.title: Europe Today: Sánchez under pressure as Europe backs Ukraine
item.description: Europe's five leading military powers meet in Berlin to discuss support for Ukraine and tougher sanctions on Russia, while US-Iran negotiations continue in Switzerland. Meanwhile, Spain's Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez faces pressure over a widening corruption scandal.

item.title: French woman allegedly held captive by husband for 12 years rescued in Pakistan
item.description: Yasmina alleges she was held along with her five children, and they were all cut off from the outside world.

item.title: Posts misrepresent scuffle at community event to fuel anti-Rohingya hostility in Malaysia
item.description: As an online petition calling for Rohingya refugees to be removed from Malaysia in part because of "security concerns" amassed hundreds of thousands of signatures, a video was shared in posts falsely claiming it showed members of the minority group attacking embassy guards. The video shows neither Rohingya refugees nor embassy guards; it in fact shows a scuffle between Chin refugees from Myanmar and security staff outside a school in Kuala Lumpur, where the Chin Refugee Committee was holding an

item.title: Kenya to charge students with murder over deadly school fire
item.description: Sixteen pupils, aged between 15 and 18, died when a fire broke out in a dormitory at Utumishi Girls' School last month.

item.title: Brickbat: No Good Deed
item.description: Paul Powlesland led volunteers in cleaning up a polluted stream in East London, England. They worked for 10 days, removed… The post Brickbat: No Good Deed appeared first on Reason.com.

item.title: Libya’s eastern government bans entry for citizens of Sudan, Eritrea, Ethiopia and Somalia
item.description: Authorities based in the eastern part of divided Libya have banned entry for people from four nearby African countries, a move likely meant to stem the influx of migrants seeking to set off for Europe from the country's northern coast. Libya's coast is a key departure points for migrants from North Africa trying to reach European shores for a better life there. Separately, tensions have risen in the last few months in both eastern and western Libya against the resettlement of refugees from other countries.

item.title: Photos show Philippine Catholics smearing mud on their bodies for festival honoring John the Baptist
item.description: Devout Catholics smeared their bodies with mud and wore banana leaves as they participated in an annual festival in Bibiclat, a village in the Philippines, on Wednesday. The Taong Putik, or Mud People, festival has been passed on from generation to generation in the community as a way of showing devotion to the village's patron saint, St. John the Baptist. Local church leaders say the tradition dates back to the 1800s, when farmers smeared themselves with mud as an expression of humility and covered themselves with banana leaves to conceal their identities.

item.title: Philippine devotees honor St. John the Baptist with a mud-covered display of faith
item.description: Hundreds of Catholic devotees wrapped themselves in dried banana leaves and covered their bodies with mud on Wednesday in the Philippine village of Bibiclat, taking part in a display of faith honoring St. John the Baptist. The Taong Putik, or Mud People, festival is held annually in this village in Asia’s largest Catholic nation as devotees thank the local patron saint for miracles and fulfill vows made in prayer. Melencio Nenuda, a 39-year-old construction worker, said the mud-covered parishioners frightened him as a child and he used to hide when they passed by.

item.title: Britain must engage again with the Taliban
item.description: Afghanistan remains an emotional subject. A twenty-year campaign costing billions. The loss of 457 British servicemen and women. Thousands more returning wounded, physically and mentally. A generation of military personnel, diplomats and aid workers invested in a mission to build a stable, democratic state.

item.title: Peru right-wing presidential hopeful Fujimori appears poised to win runoff
item.description: Peru's right-wing presidential candidate Keiko Fujimori built what may be an unassailable lead as vote counting for the runoff election entered its final stages, official figures showed.

item.title: German railway says component swap led to outage that stranded travelers nationwide
item.description: A late-night communications system outage that left trains and travelers stranded around Germany was caused by workers swapping out a component, the network operator said Wednesday as it faced criticism and questions over the embarrassing chaos. The main railway operator, federal government-owned Deutsche Bahn, also apologized for the abrupt halt in services late Tuesday, when all trains in Germany ground to a stop. Long lines formed at information desks as travelers tried to figure out how to reach their destination and where to spend the night.

item.title: On This Day, June 24: Isner defeats Mahut in longest tennis match
item.description: On June 24, 2010, John Isner defeated Nicolas Mahut in a first-round match played over three days at Wimbledon.

item.title: North Korean soldier held after crossing border into South Korea
item.description: South Korean authorities have taken a North Korean soldier into custody after he crossed the heavily fortified inter-Korean border, the Yonhap news agency reported on Wednesday, citing South Korea's military. The crossing is believed to have been a defection from the North, according to Yonhap.

item.title: These traditionalist Catholics are defying Pope Leo XIV, and embracing their outsider status
item.description: Rather than shying away from the confrontation, the Society of St. Pius X seems intent on embracing its notoriety. The group, which celebrates the traditional Latin Mass and rejects the modernizing reforms of the Catholic Church, is planning a highly organized, four-day, livestreamed extravaganza for the consecrations at its Swiss seminary — complete with a souvenir wine set offered to those attending. The July 1 event, nearly four decades after the group first became a thorn in the Vatican's side, suggests it is leaning in even more ardently to its schismatic status for a new generation of Catholics who prefer their Masses in Latin and don’t mind that their bishops are out of communion with Rome.

item.title: Nurse, 41, Dies on Operating Table During Surgery to Remove Toxic Butt Fillers
item.description: A 41-year-old nursing assistant died on the operating table on June 18 during surgery to remove toxic biopolymer fillers from her buttocks at a clinic inside a shopping center, authorities… The post Nurse, 41, Dies on Operating Table During Surgery to Remove Toxic Butt Fillers appeared first on Where Is The Buzz | Breaking News, Entertainment, Exclusive Interviews & More.

item.title: Fears grow for civilians as paramilitary group closes in on strategic Sudanese city
item.description: Paramilitary attacks on a strategic city of a half-million people in central Sudan have raised international alarm that another round of mass violence against civilians is being planned as the country's war surges into its fourth year. “We must not allow the horrors of El Fasher to be repeated in El Obeid,” a spokesman for U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres said in a statement. More than 6,000 people were killed in three days last year when the Rapid Support Forces seized el-Fasher in an attack that U.N. experts said bore the “hallmarks of genocide.”

item.title: North Korea jump-starts naval buildup by commissioning its largest-ever warship
item.description: North Korea on Tuesday commissioned its largest-ever warship, a 5,000-ton destroyer that military analysts say could give Pyongyang’s adversaries something more to think about in a time of crisis.

item.title: China premier urges AI governance to avoid 'losing control'
item.description: The world risks "losing control" of frontier technology such as artificial intelligence if governments are too slow to regulate it, China's premier warned attendees at "Summer Davos" on Wednesday."However, we cannot ignore increasingly prominent risks of losing control of technology and ethical lapses," he said.

item.title: Thousands stranded as technical malfunction temporarily halts entire German railway network
item.description: Deutsche Bahn said its services were resuming “step by step” after an almost three hour outage which froze all of its operations nationwide.

item.title: Second Australian state reports bird flu case; Papua New Guinea blocks poultry imports
item.description: SYDNEY, June 24 (Reuters) - Australian authorities ramped up surveillance and testing after a second state reported a case of the highly pathogenic H5N1 bird flu, while neighbouring Papua New Guinea

item.title: Retail As Critical Infrastructure In Finland’s AI-Era Defense Strategy
item.description: Finland’s Total Defense model shows how AI and retail infrastructure combine to strengthen national resilience and supply chain security.

item.title: Diabetes patients in Gaza face survival battle amid war shortages
item.description: Insulin and equipment shortages in Gaza pose fatal risks, leading to critical health complications.

item.title: Necessity drives gold miners in DR Congo's Ebola epicentre
item.description: In the mines of Mongbwalu, the epicentre of an Ebola outbreak in the northeastern Democratic Republic Congo, gold prospectors have no choice but to overcome their fear of the virus and carry on their backbreaking work.The DRC's 17th Ebola outbreak was declared on May 15 after several unexplained deaths in Mongbwalu in the mineral-rich but volatile Ituri province.

item.title: Fact Check: FAKE Video Of Hands Sticking Out Of The Ground Does NOT Show People Allegedly Buried Alive In Sudan
item.description: Does a real video show the hands of hundreds of people who were buried alive in Sudan? No, that's not true: The content detection tool at Hive Moderation determined the video was 81.6% likely to be AI-generated. A visual analysis...

item.title: UK-bound Manx students to be offered meningitis jabs
item.description: Manx students heading to UK universities in September will be offered two-dose MenB jabs.

item.title: A Serbian town is known for raspberries that are exported around the world
item.description: Serbia 's town of Arilje is known as the Balkan country's “raspberry capital,” with its fame reaching beyond borders. Arilje and its surroundings yield berries that are exported as far as the United States and Japan. Serbia is among the top three exporters of raspberries globally.

item.title: Lebanese on the edge of Israel's occupation live with fear and rising tensions
item.description: Looking out from a friend’s balcony, Milia el-Cheikh struggled to find her own home in the ruins of her now-deserted village, its entrances strung with barbed wire. Israel has occupied vast areas and fighting has raged through declared ceasefires. El-Cheikh, one of the few Christians from Dibbine, found shelter in another village but regularly visits Jdeidat Marjayoun, a mostly Christian village next to her hometown, to have coffee with a friend from church.

item.title: First black British pilot celebrated in exhibition
item.description: Born in Southampton in 1892 Alexander Patterson's rose to the rank of officer in 1917.

item.title: The pointless world of Unesco
item.description: Certain accolades never fail to inspire confidence in travellers: whether it’s Michelin stars for restaurants or – less glamorously – Atol protection for package holidays. But does the concept of Unesco World Heritage deserve a place on that hallowed list?

item.title: Britain’s top forensic scientist: ‘Something went wrong’ in the Lucy Letby trial
item.description: Prof Lady Sue Black, Baroness Black of Strome – her official title – is one of the world’s leading forensic scientists. As a professor of anatomy and forensic anthropologist, she has identified human remains in the aftermath of war and disaster all over the world, and her research has led to the convictions of war criminals, paedophiles and murderers. She has written several books, hosted the BBC documentary series History Cold Case and collaborated with crime writers, including her friend Val M

item.title: Fact Check: FAKE Video Shows Orthodox Priests Blessing Gas Station Pump -- It Is AI-Generated
item.description: Is this a real video of Orthodox priests blessing a gas station pump? No, that's not true: An AI detection tool determined that the video was 100% "likely to be AI-generated" and does not appear to depict a genuine event....

item.title: In a rainbow-trimmed robe at the World Cup, a gay Qatari doctor advocates for equal rights
item.description: Formally dressed in a traditional Qatari bisht, Dr. Nasser Mohamed strolled past a crowd of several hundred people outside Chase Center as the England-Croatia World Cup match was shown high above on the big screen. “That’s why the World Cup is really powerful, because people don’t need to hear about who I am — I can just walk, be seen, and that’s it,” he said. Four years ago, when the World Cup was played in his home country and Mohamed was already living across the world in San Francisco, he came out and became an exceptionally rare openly gay man from Qatar, where gay sex is prohibited and he can't present femininely.

item.title: AI cut the UK's war planning cycle from 72 hours to just one, British Army's top general says
item.description: "What they're going to do with the other 71 hours, I don't know," said Gen. Roly Walker, adding that a corps could now hit 10 times as many targets.

item.title: Japan PM heckled at WWII memorial
item.description: Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi was heckled at a World War II memorial event by protesters angry about Tokyo's further shift away from its decades-long pacifist stance, television footage showed.Following its World War II defeat in 1945, Japan has maintained a pacifist stance, with its army restricted to defensive operations.

item.title: Trial begins in fatal attack on German train conductor
item.description: The trial of a man accused of fatally assaulting a train conductor during a ticket inspection in western Germany begins on Wednesday, nearly five months after the attack. The 26-year-old Greek national is alleged to have punched the conductor in the head during a dispute aboard a regional train near the town of Landstuhl on February 2.

item.title: South African prisons open art galleries to showcase inmates' work
item.description: When most people think of prison, they picture steel bars, locked gates and lost freedom. The exhibition of inmates' artwork is part of the country’s effort to reduce repeat offending through rehabilitation programs behind bars. The Department of Correctional Services has opened nine prison arts-and-crafts galleries since 2023, aiming to help inmates develop skills, earn income and prepare for life after release.

item.title: Hanoi residents mount silent protest over home demolitions
item.description: Silent prayer vigils, cautiously worded protest banners and T-shirts pleading for neighbourhoods to be spared -- Hanoi residents are showing rare public opposition to a massive redevelopment scheme that could displace hundreds of thousands.But private grumbling has given way to tentative public opposition as residents fight to save their homes.

item.title: Bolivia's government is 'stoking a civil war,' ex-president Evo Morales tells AFP
item.description: Former Bolivian president Evo Morales told AFP in an exclusive interview on Tuesday that the South American country's government is "stoking a civil war" with its "neoliberal politics." A: With all the neoliberal politics and the colonial state, they are stoking a civil war.

item.title: Malaysia's Rohingya refugees targeted with false 2 million babies claim
item.description: Rohingyas make up the largest refugee population in Malaysia, with the group becoming frequent targets of disinformation, including recent online claims that two million babies were born from Malay-Rohingya marriages. The false posts -- which accused Rohingyas of exploiting government resources -- share a fabricated news report citing made-up population figures that an expert told AFP were "impossible".

item.title: North Korea's Kim unveils plans for 10,000-tonne warships, nuclear navy
item.description: North Korea will equip its navy with nuclear weapons and build larger warships, leader Kim Jong Un said according to state media Wednesday, as Pyongyang pushes ahead with a military expansion."The programme of equipping the Navy with nuclear weapons is following its planned course unerringly," Kim reportedly told the ceremony.

item.title: 'The sea took everything away': Nigeria's 'Happy City' is being swallowed by the ocean
item.description: "Very sad, unfortunately it's going to become more common."

item.title: Kim Jong Un calls for North Korea to build 2 large warships per year in major naval expansion push: report
item.description: Kim Jong Un calls for building two large warships annually over five years, signaling a major expansion of North Korea's naval forces and capabilities.

item.title: North Korea's Kim claims progress on nuclear-armed navy as new warship is placed into service
item.description: North Korea has commissioned a 5,000-ton destroyer that leader Kim Jong Un touts as a symbol of the country’s growing naval and nuclear capabilities, state media reported Wednesday, as Pyongyang seeks to expand its ability to project military power at sea.

item.title: Sydney woman wakes from induced coma more than a week after shark attack
item.description: Leah Stewart, 34, had one of her arms amputated after she was bitten while swimming at Coogee Beach.

item.title: Belarus's Cyber Partisans Are Taking The Fight To Russia
item.description: Cyber Partisans from Belarus helped disrupt Russian troop movements in 2022. Now the anti-Lukashenka hacker group is increasingly targeting Russia itself.

item.title: 'Carspreading' could cost major cities one in seven parking spaces
item.description: London and other major European cities risk losing up to one in seven on-street parking spaces if car sizes continue to increase at the current rate, new analysis warns. Think tank Transport & Environment (T&E), which conducted the study, also warned that the trend towards larger cars such as SUVs – which critics refer to as carspreading – could increase road deaths.

item.title: UK cracks down on Adidas, Calvin Klein, Uniqlo 'recycled' ad claims
item.description: Ads for Calvin Klein, Adidas and Uniqlo have been banned in the United Kingdom for making misleading claims about the recycled content of their clothing. The UK's Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) found all three retailers used the word "recycled" to describe their fabric or product composition but did not provide any information explaining the claim.

item.title: Honduras to buy Ukrainian drones as Kyiv's defense clout grows
item.description: Since Russia’s 2022 invasion, Ukraine has become a world leader in military drone technology, both in attack and defense.

item.title: Where’s the beer? England fans devastated as pub card machines fail
item.description: England fans struggled to buy pints while watching the World Cup as card machines failed.

item.title: North Korea should build two warships a year in next five years, Kim says
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: Countries grapple with China’s robotics lead
item.description: China’s lead in the embodied AI race is prompting major economies to adopt starkly different responses.

item.title: Germany rail network comes to complete halt nationwide due to IT malfunction
item.description: Rail company Deutsche Bahn has paused train services across the country due to a nationwide IT disruption.

item.title: Suspect in Fatal Montreal Shooting Left Behind Manifesto Linked to Incel Ideology
item.description: The purported 104-page document allegedly linked the suspect to incel ideology, advocated terror and listed pornography companies among its targets

item.title: Trains halted across Germany because of communication system problem
item.description: A problem with a communications system forced Germany’s railway system to halt all trains late Tuesday, leaving passengers stranded across the country. The main national railway operator, Deutsche Bahn, said all trains were being held at stations because of a nationwide problem with the GSM-R digital communication system, which is used for internal communication on the railway network. Deutsche Bahn said in a statement at midnight, 1 1/2 hours after it first announced the problem, that the cause had been identified, but didn't specify what it was.

item.title: Dutch court keeps Greenpeace case alive after Energy Transfer bid to toss it fails
item.description: "There may be an outright conflict between the two courts in the two cases, or there may not."

item.title: UN starts evacuating 11,000 stranded sailors from Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Following the start of the US-Israel war on Iran on February 28, Tehran had effectively closed off the vital waterway.

item.title: Cubans bid farewell to revolution hero Valdes
item.description: Ex-president Raul Castro on Tuesday led tributes to one of the last leaders of Cuba's 1959 revolution, former spy chief Ramiro Valdes, who died at the weekend.Raul Castro, 95, who was recently indicted by the United States over the 1996 downing of two civilian planes, led an honor guard at a memorial service for Valdes at the ministry of the armed forces in Havana.

item.title: Israel ‘behind cyber attack on Iranian banks’
item.description: Four Iranian banks have been targeted in a cyber attack suspected to have been launched by Israel, after the US granted Tehran access to billions in frozen funds.

item.title: Authorities Seize Over 300 Snakes from Man's Home After His Electricity Use Raises Suspicion
item.description: Police said the reptiles were found stacked in plastic containers in the man's apartment following an investigation into unusually high power consumption

item.title: IDF kills Hezbollah operatives in two small incidents, but ceasefire holds
item.description: All of this was taking place just as Jerusalem and Beirut kicked off another round of direct talks in Washington addressing a wide range of issues. In two separate incidents on Tuesday afternoon, the IDF said that it had killed armed Hezbollah operatives approaching its positions in the Ali Taher Ridge, which is at the edge of Israel’s security zone.

item.title: As 105-degree heat shuts schools across India, working mothers are pushed to a breaking point
item.description: "Most heat action plans in India barely have a gender component."

item.title: 3 dead after fatal shooting near Pornhub's headquarters
item.description: A manifesto written by the suspect lists adult film companies and actors as potential targets.

item.title: People Are Talking About 15 Global Stories The US Is Missing
item.description: From families in Vietnam ordered to dig up ancestral graves for a Trump-branded golf resort, to France breeding millions of sterilized mosquitoes, to the week Washington abruptly locked the rest of the world out of America's most powerful AI, here's what the rest of the world has been watching while it barely registered in US headlines.

item.title: Video shows terrifying moments of Montreal shooting that left two victims dead as possible incel manifesto revealed
item.description: The Quebec coroner identified the alleged shooter as 25-year-old Seth Scott Hatfield, 25, of Lethbridge, Alberta

item.title: New report shows many migrant workers lacked access to shelter during Operation Roaring Lion
item.description: Local support appeared to matter. Some 69% of all respondents said they had a person or organization to turn to in an emergency, but the level varied significantly between groups.

item.title: German city tells lido to scrap controversial language entry policy
item.description: The central German city of Halle has ordered the operator of a lido to withdraw a controversial entry rule that bars visitors who cannot demonstrate sufficient German-language comprehension, city spokesman Drago Bock said on Tuesday. Bock said the city had contacted the operator by phone and in writing, with the aim of reaching an agreed solution as quickly as possible.

item.title: EU meets with Taliban about deportation
item.description: The European Union and the Taliban met in Brussels on Tuesday to discuss the return of Afghans.

item.title: Palestinian children targeted in ‘continuing genocide’, say UN investigators
item.description: Israel is targeting Palestinian children in its “ongoing genocide” in Gaza, United Nations investigators have claimed.

item.title: A Bride and Groom Were Planning Their Wedding. Now, Their Families Are Preparing Their Funerals After They Died in a Fire at Work
item.description: The families of Anamika Samanta and Neelesh Kumar met for the first time last week ahead of their fall wedding

item.title: Major fire hits telecom ministry warehouse in Beirut
item.description: A fire broke out at a warehouse belonging to Lebanon's Ministry of Telecommunications in Beirut on Tuesday, sending flames and thick plumes of black smoke into the air, witnesses said. The smell of burning plastic hung over the area as firefighters battled the blaze.

item.title: Bolivian government says cleared all protest roadblocks
item.description: No protest roadblocks remained standing in Bolivia on Tuesday, the government said, as it deployed the army to quell weeks-long demonstrations that had throttled roads nationwide.The embattled leader has held on so far, however, and on Saturday declared a state of emergency that allows him to deploy the army and outlaw demonstrations.

item.title: NATO And Ukraine Turning To Private Sector To Help Crater Russian Airfields
item.description: A new challenge offers a reward to anyone who can develop a system to deny Russia use of its airfields.

item.title: German city tells lido to scrap controversial language entry policy
item.description: The central German city of Halle has ordered the operator of a lido to withdraw a controversial entry rule that bars visitors who cannot demonstrate sufficient German-language comprehension, city spokesman Drago Bock said on Tuesday. Bock said the city had contacted the operator by phone and in writing, with the aim of reaching an agreed solution as quickly as possible.

item.title: C40 Cities sign onto Global Urban Data Centres Pact for AI buildout standards
item.description: C40 Cities said 41 mayors launched a voluntary Global Urban Data Centres Pact at London Climate Action Week.

item.title: Belgian royals roll out red carpet for Japan's emperor
item.description: Japanese Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako received a ceremonial welcome at the Royal Palace of Brussels as they began a state visit to Belgium.

item.description: People of Biafra are Entitled to Self-Determination
